concept.headline: defmodule groups named functions. def for public, defp for private. Multiple function clauses are matched top-to-bottom by pattern. Module.function() is the call syntax.
concept.explanation: Elixir code is organized into modules with defmodule. Named functions are defined with def (public) or defp (private). A function can have MULTIPLE clauses — Elixir matches top-to-bottom and executes the first matching clause. This replaces most if/case logic at the function level. The catch-all general clause (with a named variable) goes LAST. Call: ModuleName.function_name(args).
concept.syntax:
defmodule Greeter do
  # Multiple clauses — matched top to bottom
  def greet("Alice") do
    "Special greeting for Alice!"
  end

  def greet(name) do
    "Hello, #{name}!"
  end

  # Private — only callable inside Greeter
  defp secret, do: "internal only"
end

Greeter.greet("Alice")  # "Special greeting for Alice!"
Greeter.greet("Bob")    # "Hello, Bob!"
keyPoints: defmodule Name do ... end — module definition
def name(args) do ... end — public function
defp — private (only callable within the same module)
Multiple clauses — matched top-to-bottom; first match wins

demo.intro: Watch multiple function clauses match based on pattern — specific clause first, general clause last.
demo.code:
defmodule Calculator do
  def describe(0), do: "zero"
  def describe(n) when n > 0, do: "positive"
  def describe(n), do: "negative"

  def double(x), do: x * 2

  defp internal_note, do: "private"
end

IO.puts(Calculator.describe(0))
IO.puts(Calculator.describe(5))
IO.puts(Calculator.describe(-3))
IO.puts(Calculator.double(7))

explanation.what: defmodule Calculator do — opens a module named Calculator
explanation.why: Modules are the top-level organization unit in Elixir
explanation.misconception: Module names in Elixir are atoms — Calculator is actually the atom :'Elixir.Calculator'. Module names must start with an uppercase letter by convention.

explanation.what: def describe(0), do: 'zero' — first clause: matches ONLY when argument is exactly 0
explanation.why: Literal 0 in a function head acts as a pattern constraint
explanation.change: describe/1 has a clause for literal 0
explanation.intent: Show specific literal pattern in function head
explanation.misconception: 0 in the function head is a PATTERN — it means 'only match if the argument equals 0'. This is more expressive than if n == 0 do inside the body.

explanation.what: def describe(n) when n > 0 — guard clause: matches when n > 0
explanation.why: when adds a boolean condition to a clause beyond pattern shape
explanation.change: describe/1 has a guarded clause for positive numbers
explanation.intent: Show guard clause syntax with when
explanation.misconception: Guards in Elixir are limited to a safe subset of expressions (comparison, arithmetic, type checks, rem, etc.) — you cannot call arbitrary functions in a guard. This is intentional for VM pattern-matching optimization.

explanation.what: def describe(n) — catch-all: matches ANYTHING that didn't match above
explanation.why: n is just a variable — matches any argument without constraint
explanation.change: describe/1 has a catch-all clause for negatives (and any other cases)
explanation.misconception: ORDERING matters! If you placed def describe(n) FIRST, it would ALWAYS match and the specific clauses would never be reached. The Elixir compiler warns about this. Always put specific clauses before general ones.

explanation.what: def double(x), do: x * 2 — one-line function with comma + do:
explanation.why: Short functions use compact do: form instead of do...end block
explanation.misconception: def f(x), do: body is IDENTICAL to def f(x) do\n  body\nend — just more concise for single-expression functions.

explanation.what: defp internal_note — private function, only callable within Calculator module
explanation.why: defp restricts the function to internal use
explanation.misconception: Calculator.internal_note() from outside the module raises UndefinedFunctionError. defp is how you hide implementation details in Elixir — there is no class-level access control, only module-level pub/priv.

explanation.what: Calculator.describe(0) — Elixir tries clause 1: describe(0) matches literal 0
explanation.misconception: Elixir runtime checks clauses IN ORDER. Clause 1: (0) matches? YES — execute immediately, skip clauses 2 and 3.
The clause matching happens at CALL TIME, not compile time. Elixir generates efficient dispatch code, but logically it evaluates clauses top-to-bottom.

explanation.what: Calculator.describe(5) — clause 1: 5 != 0, skip. Clause 2: n=5, 5>0? YES
explanation.misconception: Clause 1 (literal 0) is skipped first. THEN clause 2's guard is evaluated: 5 > 0 is true.

explanation.why: Clause 2 guard matched n > 0 for n=5
explanation.misconception: All integers > 0 will match clause 2 — 1, 100, 9999, etc.

explanation.what: Calculator.describe(-3) — clause 1: skip. Clause 2: -3>0? NO — skip. Clause 3: catch-all matches.
explanation.why: Neither specific clause matched; catch-all clause 3 fires
explanation.misconception: If clause 3 did NOT exist and -3 was passed, Elixir would raise FunctionClauseError — no matching clause. Elixir requires all inputs to be handled.
The catch-all (n without guard) actually matches zero too if we removed clause 1 — it's truly 'any n'. The ordering of clauses is what makes the logic correct.
Calculator.double calls a PUBLIC function from outside the module. Calculator.internal_note() would fail with UndefinedFunctionError.

prediction.question:
Which clause fires?

defmodule Greeter do
  def greet("Alice"), do: "Hi Alice!"
  def greet(name),    do: "Hello, #{name}!"
end

Greeter.greet("Bob")
prediction.explanation: Clause 1 checks: 'Bob' == 'Alice'? No — skip. Clause 2: name='Bob', matches. Returns 'Hello, Bob!'.

explanation.afterDemo: Multiple function clauses are the Elixir way of writing conditional logic at the function boundary rather than inside the function body. This leads to smaller, clearer functions — each clause handles one specific case. Combined with pattern matching in function heads, this is one of the most expressive and readable patterns in Elixir.
keyTakeaways: defmodule / def / defp — module, public function, private function.
Multiple clauses matched top-to-bottom. Specific first, general last.
when guards add boolean conditions. FunctionClauseError if no clause matches.

practice.prompt: Define defmodule Shape with def area({:circle, r}) returning 3.14159 * r * r and def area({:square, s}) returning s * s. Call both.
practice.starterCode:
defmodule Shape do
  def area({:circle, r}), do: 3.14159 * r * r
  def area({:square, s}), do: s * s
end

IO.puts(Shape.area({:circle, 5}))
IO.puts(Shape.area({:square, 4}))
practice.solutionCode:
defmodule Shape do
  def area({:circle, r}), do: 3.14159 * r * r
  def area({:square, s}), do: s * s
end

IO.puts(Shape.area({:circle, 5}))
IO.puts(Shape.area({:square, 4}))
practice.hint: Function head patterns {:circle, r} and {:square, s} dispatch to different area formulas.

retryHints: def area({:circle, r}) dispatches on the :circle atom

summary.title: Modules & Named Functions — Complete
takeaways: defmodule groups functions. def public, defp private.
Multiple clauses match top-to-bottom. Specific clauses before catch-all.
when guards add boolean conditions to match beyond shape.
